text: The surgeon performs a left lower lobectomy, encountering pleural adhesions at the site of the previous mini thoracotomy, which are dissected using a blunt instrument or hook. The lung is visible post-adhesiolysis. The pulmonary ligament is dissected, and the station IX lymph node is harvested. The inferior border of the inferior pulmonary vein is identified, followed by the sectioning of the anterior and posterior mediastinal pleura to reveal the superior border of the vein, preparing for its dissection. The vein is stapled after completing the arterial dissection to prevent vascular tension.

The surgeon releases the fissure using a hook and scissors, revealing the pulmonary artery within the fissure. The interlobar lymph node is visible between the lower lobe bronchus and branches of the pulmonary artery, including A-V, A-VI, and basal truncus.

The surgeon performs a difficult dissection of the lymphadenopathy due to tight adhesions to bronchial and vascular structures, using both blunt dissection and hook electrocautery. After freeing the arterial branches from the lymph node, a 35mm endostapler cartridge is applied to each vessel for vascular dissection. The lymph node and surrounding structures, including the left lower bronchus and pulmonary artery, are visible. A curved tip stapler reload is used for the inferior pulmonary vein, revealing the vein stump and left lower lobe bronchus. The bronchus is released by completing the interlobar lymphadenectomy and finalizing its dissection with a 45mm green endostapler reload.

The surgeon checks the inflation of the upper lobe, indicated by a blue arrow, before firing the stapler. The mediastinal and hilar lymphadenectomy is completed, and a 24 French Sharier chest tube is inserted to drain the pleural cavity through the working incision. A postoperative chest X-ray shows a nearly normal appearance.
